title: Legacy vs. Scale: The Real Test of Leadership
excerpt: Being close and simple is not a virtue in itself. Having wide impact is not always arrogance. The problem lies in motivation, not in scale.
content:
I was reading Erich Fromm recently and got stuck at one point.

Fromm presents the baker who makes the finest bread as a productive, fulfilled individual — close, simple, concrete, egoless. But he looks with suspicion at leaders who serve wider communities: "Is this ego? Is this ambition?" he asks.

I understood his point. But I respectfully disagree.

Being close and simple is not a virtue in itself. Having wide impact is not always arrogance.

The problem lies in motivation, not in scale.

The baker's contribution is beautiful — but its effect on generations and communities is limited. It rarely goes beyond personal satisfaction, and once that moment passes, it leaves no trace.

But someone who builds tunnels, roads, and railways across 7 countries cannot simply be dismissed as "ambitious." The people who pass through those structures don't know you. You don't know them. Yet you are touching their lives. They will reach their families faster and more safely. Goods will be transported at lower cost. An entire country's economy will be shaped. For generations.

The Real Question

The real question is not about scale — it's about legacy: When the work is done, what remains — only personal satisfaction, or a lasting mark on the world? Any discussion that avoids this question remains incomplete.

title: The Most Dangerous Person on Any Project
excerpt: Managing complex projects across 17 nationalities teaches you fast: the most dangerous person is not the one who admits uncertainty. It's the one who never does.
content:
Managing complex projects across multiple countries, with teams from 17 nationalities, teaches you something fast:

The most dangerous person on a project is not the one who admits uncertainty. It's the one who never does.

I've seen it in boardrooms. I've seen it underground, 40 meters below surface, where overconfidence has real consequences.

The "Peak of Mount Stupid" is not just a concept. In infrastructure, it shows up as:

— Underestimated risk assessments
— Rushed schedules presented with false precision
— Junior engineers who've read the manual but haven't faced the ground conditions

The Valley of Despair

The Valley of Despair? That's where real professionals are forged. The ones who say "Trust me, it's complicated" — those are the people we want in our team.

True expertise is humble. It asks more questions than it answers.

Where do you see this curve most clearly in your field?

title: Hindsight Bias: The Silent Killer of Institutional Learning
excerpt: The hindsight bias doesn't just distort memory — it quietly kills institutional learning. When outcomes rewrite the past, mistakes cannot be diagnosed and decision quality does not improve.
content:
A while ago, I wrote about the "I knew it all along" reflex — how easy it is to sound wise after outcomes are known.

Recently, revisiting Daniel Kahneman's book Thinking Fast and Slow reminded me why that reflex is not harmless, but deeply corrosive for organizations.

The hindsight bias doesn't just distort memory — it quietly kills institutional learning.

Because when outcomes rewrite the past:
— Mistakes cannot be diagnosed properly
— Decision quality does not improve
— Everyone absolves themselves by saying "I knew it"

At that point, the issue is no longer technical — it is a leadership problem.

Kahneman's Argument

In Thinking, Fast and Slow, Chapter 22, Kahneman questions "expert intuition" for exactly this reason. In many domains, people do not rely on intuition — they rely on stories reconstructed after the fact, once uncertainty has disappeared. That is how overconfidence gets polished into fake expertise.

Real foresight is not speaking after the outcome. Real foresight is taking responsibility while uncertainty still exists.

Organizations learn not by asking "Who was right?" but by asking "What did we know, what did we not know, and why did we decide anyway?"

Otherwise, we don't learn. We just become better storytellers.

title: The Purloined Letter: Why Better Mental Models Beat More Data
excerpt: In most complex problems, what truly matters is not information, but the ability to correctly model how the other side sees the world. Poe knew this in 1844.
content:
Difficult problems are sometimes solved only with a better mental model.

In 1844, Edgar Allan Poe published a short story that still offers powerful lessons for business and leadership today: The Purloined Letter.

The Story

A sensitive letter is stolen by a clever minister who understands its power as blackmail. The police search his residence again and again — secret compartments, drawers, wall cavities. Everything is torn apart. Yet the letter is nowhere to be found. Because the police operate under a single assumption: "Something this important must be very well hidden."

This is where C. Auguste Dupin enters the scene. What sets him apart is not access to information, but his ability to understand how people think.

Dupin asks: "What would this man do, knowing how I am going to think?"

The answer is clear: the letter is not hidden. It is in plain sight — hanging in a rack on the wall, right where no one thought it important enough to truly see.

The Business Lesson

This is exactly where we fail most often in business, negotiations, and crisis situations:

— We collect more data.
— We perform more analysis.
— We produce thicker reports.

Yet we misread the other side's mental assumptions. And while the solution is right in front of us, we keep searching hidden compartments.

The hardest problems are not solved with more information, but with a better mental model.

title: Some Races Never End — Only the Baton Changes Hands
excerpt: Kathrine Switzer resisted, finished, got disqualified — then ran until women were officially accepted. Marie Curie kept running too. Today, my daughter Alara doesn't need permission to run.
content:
I discovered Kathrine Switzer through Özgür Karcıoğlu's LinkedIn article.

In 1967's Boston Marathon, an official tried to rip off her number because women were "too fragile" for marathons. She resisted, finished the race, got disqualified — then ran persistently until women were officially accepted in 1972.

Marie Skłodowska-Curie faced similar barriers. The only woman studying science at Sorbonne in 1891, initially excluded from her 1903 Nobel (added only through her husband's insistence), rejected by the French Academy even after her second Nobel in 1911.

She kept running: two Nobels, radium and polonium discoveries, wartime X-ray units.

Because they ran, Alara doesn't need permission to run.

Today, my daughter Alara Tuncer pursues her doctorate at University of Toronto Sunnybrook, walking the path they opened. But she still runs every day.

Some races never end — only the baton changes hands.

Kathrine Switzer'ı Özgür Karcıoğlu'nun LinkedIn makalesiyle tanıdım. 1967 Boston Maratonu'nda bir görevli numarasını söküp atmaya çalıştı — kadınlar "çok narin"di. Direndi, bitirdi, diskalifiye edildi — sonra 1972'de kadınlar resmen kabul edilene kadar ısrarla koştu.

Marie Skłodowska-Curie benzer engelleri yaşadı. 1891'de Sorbonne'da fizik okuyan tek kadın, 1903 Nobel'inden başta dışlandı, 1911'de ikinci Nobel'inden sonra bile Fransız Akademisi kapılarını açmadı.

Bugün kızım Alara Tuncer, University of Toronto Sunnybrook'da doktorasını yapıyor. Onlar koştuğu için Alara'nın izne ihtiyacı yok. Ama hâlâ her gün koşuyor. Bazı yarışlar hiç bitmiyor, sadece bayrak değişiyor.

title: PolonceKart: Redesigning Language Learning
excerpt: Flashcard apps are old — but when done right, they're still the most effective learning tool. Why I built PolonceKart from scratch, and the power of a single HTML file.
content:
Gülay and I are learning Polish. From the same teacher, at the same pace. To make this process more effective, I looked for a tool — and when I couldn't find one, I built it.

Why a Single HTML File?

PolonceKart is published on Netlify as a single HTML file. No installation, no account, no app download. One URL — that's it. This simplicity is not a constraint, it's a choice.

The best tool is the one that gets used. Not the perfect app with a complex setup, but the simple tool that's always accessible.

CSV-based vocabulary management means adding new words takes minutes. Ko-fi integration enables community support. English and Turkish interface options expand the reach.

The Psychology of Language Learning

I apply Kahneman here too: repetition to train System 1, context to activate System 2. Not just memorizing words, but seeing words inside a sentence — this difference determines long-term memory.

title: İtibar tanınmak ile başlar
excerpt: İtibar inşa edilir, korunur ve başkalarının sizin hakkında söyledikleri ile yükselir. Piramidin temelinden zirvesine: Tanınma, Güven, İlişki, Memnuniyet, Bağlılık, Elçi.
content:
Biraz önce EKOTÜRK kanalında Alkın Lakin programının yeniden gösteriminde Emre Alkin'den İtibar Piramidi'ni dinledim.

İtibar tanınmakla başlar...

İtibar tanınmak ile başlar, piramidin üst basamaklarına doğru güven — ilişki — memnuniyet — bağlılık — elçi olarak devam eder. Güven verdiğiniz ve kendinizi kanıtladığınız ölçüde itibarınız artar.

En sonunda sizin kendi hakkınızda değil, başkalarının sizin hakkınızda söyledikleri kadar itibarınız olur.

Piramidin temelinden zirvesine:

  • Tanınma — Görünür olmak
  • Güven — Söz ve eylem birliği
  • İlişki — Bağ kurma yeteneği
  • Memnuniyet — Değer yaratma
  • Bağlılık — Sürdürülebilir etki
  • Elçi — Başkalarının sizi savunması

Bence "Güven — İlişki — Memnuniyet" üçlüsü aslında birbirini besleyen ve birlikte çalışan bir bütün. Güven olmadan sağlıklı ilişki kurulamaz, ilişki olmadan memnuniyet yaratılamaz. Bu üç basamak itibarın omurgasını oluşturuyor.

Tanınma'dan Güven-İlişki-Memnuniyet grubuna geçiş ne kadar zor ise, buradan Bağlılık'a atlamak da o ölçüde zaman ve çaba ister. Her geçiş, sabır ve tutarlılık gerektirir.

İtibar inşa edilir, korunur ve başkalarının sizin hakkında söyledikleri ile yükselir.

title: While We Rest, They Build
excerpt: Tunnel construction never sleeps. The work continues through the night, in conditions most of us will never experience.
content:
This photo came through from our site tonight at 21:59 — an engineer leaving an exhausting day at the tunnel portal, long after most of us have settled into our warm homes.

Tunnel construction never sleeps. The work continues through the night, in conditions most of us will never experience. The cold, the darkness, the isolation of working at mountains but underground — these are the realities our tunnel teams face every single day.

Behind every meter of progress, there are people like the one in this photo; dedicated professionals who trade comfort for commitment, who understand that infrastructure projects of this scale demand sacrifice.

To our tunnel crews working tonight in North Macedonia, and to all the men and women who keep our projects moving forward while the rest of the world sleeps — your dedication doesn't go unnoticed.

This is what it takes to build the connections that will serve nations for generations.

title: Taking Risks in Business
excerpt: Taking Risks in Business: Lessons from the Racing Track...
content:
Taking Risks in Business: Lessons from the Racing Track

One of motor racing's legendary pilots, Ayrton Senna, had a very famous quote: "If you no longer go for a gap that exists, you are no longer a racing driver." This quote perfectly summarizes not just the mentality of racing drivers, but also the mindset needed for business success — seizing opportunities and taking calculated risks.

Here's a perfect example that illustrates both the potential and the peril of this philosophy: Belgian driver Laurens Vanthoor's incident at Germany's legendary Nürburgring circuit.

The Nordschleife section of the Nürburgring is called the "Green Hell" — a 20-kilometer narrow, dangerous track. The Dottinger Höhe straight is the most critical section where pilots reach speeds over 300 km/h. Here, Vanthoor spotted a chance to overtake two slow cars in heavy traffic. Just as Senna advocated, he saw the gap and went for it. He began the maneuver with surgical precision but made slight contact with another car. This minor contact, which would be insignificant under normal circumstances, created a domino effect at that high speed — the tire blew out, Vanthoor lost control, and the Audi crashed hard into the barriers. Fortunately, thanks to modern safety technologies, the pilot escaped unharmed, but the car was completely unusable and the team had to retire from the race.

The Business Parallel

This story perfectly mirrors business decision-making. Just like in racing, in business you must seize opportunities when you see them — but the key question is: are you aware of the risks you're taking?

Senna was right — you have to go for the gaps. But here's what separates great leaders from reckless ones: the best leaders, like the best drivers, don't avoid risks — they take intelligent ones with full awareness of what's at stake.

What gaps are you considering in your business? How are you calculating the risk?

  • Are you aware of the risks you're taking?
  • Could this decision cause unexpected damage to your company?
  • If things go wrong, what's the impact on your team, your health, your mental well-being?
  • Are you taking calculated risks or chasing opportunities blindly?

title: Understanding Immune Memory
excerpt: HEB protein acts like a wise CEO who restrains the urge to overreact to every market fluctuation.
content:
Understanding Immune Memory: A Business Lens on Cutting-Edge Research

Understanding how our immune system forms memory is one of the most critical areas in modern medicine, and even perhaps in business.

The research "HEB Restrains Effector Gene Expression during Early CD8+ Memory Precursor T Cell Differentiation," which my daughter Alara Sedef Tuncer contributed to, reveals an important discovery.

Simply, without the HEB protein, T cells respond very rapidly but lose their long-term memory capacity.

An Analogy from My World

HEB protein acts like a wise CEO who restrains the urge to overreact to every market fluctuation. Without HEB, T cells become like startups that burn through resources quickly for immediate impact but fail to build sustainable competitive advantages.

This is a resource allocation problem every business leader recognizes — the classic trade-off between short-term performance and long-term capability building.

This discovery could revolutionize how we approach immune system diseases and vaccine development — much like how understanding customer lifetime value transformed marketing strategies. Nature always shows us the way.

Seeing our young researchers tackle such fundamental questions fills me with pride! 👏

title: Challenges will grow, but we will always find ways to compete
excerpt: No matter how much our challenges grow, we will always find ways to overcome this challenge via innovations.
content:
I remember when I was young ( not so far in history ) I was travelling from my home city to İstanbul for business. I was taking an early bus from Ankara and arriving to Istanbul around 10:00. I was attending one or maximum two meetings, and then taking the return bus sometime around 16:00 arriving home at 21:00. I have been physically very tired, sleeping deeply, and having the minutes of meetings first thing next day.

After 20 years, the world changed a lot and my routine became absurdly more demanding though I am getting old and tired. Today, I need to take an early flight, working on the plane with my laptop or phablet, answering emails immediately on the way. I need to have several meetings, even having some talks on the way. Then taking the latest flight back, writing minutes on the way back in the plane. Reaching my home dead tired.

My old routine was physically challenging but much comfortable mentally. Now it is physically less demanding, but lot more challenging mentally.

Fortunately, it is expected many technologies like virtual reality and big data will be part of our daily life, similar to cloud today. We will see less hardware improvements but huge software innovations in 2017, which will support us overcoming our challenges.

No matter how much our challenges grow, we will always find ways to overcome this challenge via innovations..

I wish a very prosperous and innovative future to all of you.
